examples.title: Universal Turing Machine Simulation
examples.description: Simulates any Turing machine M on input w to show Lμ is r.e.
examples.code:
FUNCTION UniversalTM(⟨M⟩, w):
  // ⟨M⟩ is encoding of Turing machine M
  // w is input string
  
  DECODE ⟨M⟩ to obtain transition function δ
  INITIALIZE tape with w
  SET current state to initial state of M
  
  WHILE true:
    IF current state is accept state of M:
      RETURN ACCEPT
    IF current state is reject state of M:
      // For r.e. - loops on rejection (doesn't halt here)
      LOOP_FOREVER
    
    READ symbol at current tape position
    IF δ(current state, symbol) is undefined:
      LOOP_FOREVER
    
    (new_state, new_symbol, direction) = δ(current_state, symbol)
    WRITE new_symbol at current position
    MOVE head in direction
    SET current_state = new_state
END

// This shows Lμ is recursively enumerable (semi-decidable)
// Accepts if M accepts w, loops otherwise
examples.explanation: The universal language Lμ is r.e. because we can construct this UTM that accepts if M accepts w. It halts on acceptance but may loop on rejection, demonstrating r.e. but not recursive nature.

examples.title: Decider for Recursive Language Union
examples.description: Shows recursive languages are closed under union
examples.explanation: Shows that union of two recursive languages is recursive. We run both deciders in parallel and accept if either accepts. Both always halt, so the union decider always halts.

examples.title: Enumeration Procedure for R.E. Languages
examples.description: Shows how to enumerate strings in an r.e. language
examples.explanation: This enumeration procedure demonstrates that a language is recursively enumerable iff there exists an enumeration procedure that eventually outputs each string in the language (may run forever but will list all strings in L).
